rationale_for_baseline_tier: Subluxations that have spontaneously reduced, with no stuck-out deformity and intact distal neurovascular status, are an hEDS instability flare — not an ED-by-ambulance emergency — but joints/neurovascular status can't be examined remotely and the patient is immobile and isolated (fall/self-care risk), so urgent in-person review (± imaging) with analgesia/bracing/physiotherapy and fall-safety (T3) is proportionate. T2 is the minimum viable pass. Escalate to T5 for a joint stuck out/deformed or a cold/blue/numb limb.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A joint slips out and stays out/deformed and won't go back, or a limb becomes cold, blue, pale or numb
escalation_edges.rationale: A persistent dislocation needs reduction; neurovascular compromise is limb-threatening.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If a joint slips out and stays out or deformed and won't go back, or your hand/foot turns cold, blue or numb — go to ED or call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever or a single joint becoming hot, red and very swollen
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to ED / call for urgent care
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a fever or one joint becomes hot, red and very swollen, go to ED / seek urgent care.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to get off the floor or reach food/water safely while alone
escalation_edges.rationale: Immobility while isolated risks a long lie/dehydration.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't safely get up or reach water, call someone to help you — or an ambulance if you're stuck and can't manage.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone, immobile on the floor/couch
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Arrange practical help to transfer and reach fluids; consider ambulance if stuck.
Bracing and hypermobility physiotherapy for recurrence prevention.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Your loose joints slipped out and popped back in, and the muscles have cramped up to protect them — an hEDS flare. Because they're back in place and your hand/foot are warm and working, they don't need putting back. Rest them supported in a mid-range position, use braces, take simple painkillers within limits, and avoid extreme movements. Arrange an in-person review and some help to get off the floor and reach water.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent in-person GP review within 1–2 days (± imaging) plus physiotherapy; use ED/000 for the features below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: Go to ED/000 for a joint stuck out/deformed or a cold/blue/numb limb; arrange same-day help if you can't get up safely.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the reduced shoulder/patella subluxations with reactive spasm, intact neurovascular status, Beighton-confirmed hypermobility, and the need for joint review/bracing/physiotherapy and fall-safety.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Managing/reassuring with no dislocation/neurovascular screen or fall-safety, missing a persistent dislocation or neurovascular compromise.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A persistent dislocation with neurovascular compromise is missed and the limb is injured, or the patient has a long lie while stuck alone.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Ambulance/000 for reduced subluxations with intact neurovascular status, over-medicalising a manageable flare.
